text.title: Indie Mini-CRM (Notion) Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Indie Mini-CRM (Notion) application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Workspace MUST be exactly 4 databases (Contacts, Outreach, Partners, Touches) with linked relations. Single-table CRMs are rejected.
rule.rationale: Indie flows are many-to-many; a single table collapses past ~50 contacts.

rule.statement: Contacts.Type MUST be a closed select (subscriber / customer / partner / prospect / VIP). Free-text type fields are rejected.
rule.rationale: Open enums become folklore; closed enums make filter views deterministic.

rule.statement: Every contact MUST carry a last_touch_date populated by ritual. Missing dates are rejected.
rule.rationale: Stalling detection depends on the date; without it the CRM becomes a graveyard.

rule.statement: Spec MUST schedule the 3 rituals (morning outreach batch, weekly partner review, monthly community pulse) as recurring tasks.
rule.rationale: Unscheduled rituals decay within 4 weeks; calendar entries protect them.

rule.statement: Outreach DM / email MUST use templates with response-rate tracking columns. Free-form outreach is rejected.
rule.rationale: Without templates response rate cannot be optimised; templates also halve drafting cost.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
